title: Kuznetsov's Calabi-Yau categories: introduction and applications
speaker: Daniel Huybrechts
tags: Homological Mirror Symmetry: Methods and Structures

proof_stages.label: Kuznetsov component and fractional Calabi–Yau theorem
proof_stages.summary: For a smooth degree-d hypersurface X, the Kuznetsov component A_X is defined as the right orthogonal to the exceptional line bundles in D^b(X), Serre functors are recalled, and Kuznetsov’s theorem states that A_X has a fractional Calabi–Yau Serre functor with dimension governed by the same number sigma that appears as the socle degree of the Jacobian ring.

proof_stages.label: Degree-shift functor and key lemma reduction
proof_stages.summary: Using the admissible embedding A_X into D^b(X), the projection of twisting by O(1) defines the degree-shift autoequivalence on A_X, and the key lemma that its d-th power is the shift [2] is shown to imply, by Serre duality, that the cubic fourfold component is Calabi–Yau of dimension 2.

proof_stages.label: Fourier–Mukai kernel proof of the key lemma
proof_stages.summary: The product subcategory A_X  A_X is used to represent endofunctors of A_X by projected Fourier–Mukai kernels, and a derived pullback/Koszul calculation for the ambient diagonal proves the kernel isomorphism j^*O_(d)  j^*O_[2], yielding the degree-shift lemma.

proof_stages.label: Universal obstruction and first Jacobian contribution
proof_stages.summary: The diagonal boundary morphism used in the kernel proof is identified with the universal obstruction to extending objects of D^b(X) to the first infinitesimal neighborhood of X, and its factorization through the universal Atiyah and Kodaira–Spencer classes produces a map from H^1(T_X), classically the degree-d part of the Jacobian ring, into Hochschild homology of A_X.

proof_stages.label: Jacobian reconstruction from the shifted category
proof_stages.summary: A joint theorem constructs a natural surjection from the full Jacobian ring J(X) onto the Hochschild homology generated by the degree-shift kernels of the pair (A_X,(1)), an isomorphism in the genuine Calabi–Yau cases d | n+2, and this gives reconstruction of X via Mather–Yau when an equivalence of Kuznetsov components respects the degree shift.

proof_stages.label: Cubic periods inside generalized K3 moduli
proof_stages.summary: The period domain of cubic fourfolds is placed inside the Mukai-lattice period domain of generalized K3 surfaces, and results of Addington–Thomas and Huybrechts, together with derived global Torelli and autoequivalence control for very general twisted K3 surfaces, translate Hodge-theoretic coincidences into derived equivalences of the associated K3 categories.

proof_stages.label: Derived-categorical proof of cubic Torelli and outlook
proof_stages.summary: Combining the categorical Hodge comparison, K3 derived Torelli, control of autoequivalences, the degree-shift-compatible Jacobian reconstruction, and Mather–Yau yields a derived-categorical proof of global Torelli for cubic fourfolds, with the same numerology suggesting possible higher-dimensional applications.

examples.name: Calabi–Yau hypersurface of degree n+2
examples.note: The case d=n+2 makes the canonical bundle trivial and the Serre functor just a shift, illustrating why Kuznetsov’s fractional Calabi–Yau terminology generalizes the ordinary Calabi–Yau case.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes the Serre-functor formula to degree n+2, uses the canonical bundle being trivial, and identifies the functor/dimension in this case

passes.reason: Degree n+2 is the critical Calabi–Yau boundary for hypersurfaces in projective space, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
degree n+2 is the canonical-trivial boundary for hypersurfaces, and it is used only as the standard Calabi–Yau Serre-functor case.
It is the critical degree d=n+2 hypersurface with trivial canonical bundle, at the Fano/Calabi–Yau boundary, and is used illustratively rather than adversarially.
Degree n+2 is the Calabi–Yau boundary for hypersurfaces, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
Degree n+2 is the adjunction-critical Calabi–Yau boundary for hypersurfaces, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
Degree n+2 is the anticanonical Calabi–Yau boundary for projective hypersurfaces, and it is used only as an illustrative case of the Serre functor.
It is used illustratively as the boundary degree d=n+2 where a hypersurface becomes Calabi–Yau.
It is used as the canonical Calabi–Yau boundary case d=n+2 where the Serre functor becomes a shift, not to refute a claim.
It is used as the standard Calabi–Yau boundary case d=n+2 where the canonical bundle becomes trivial, not to refute a claim.
it is used as the standard Calabi–Yau boundary case d=n+2 where the Serre functor is just a shift, not to refute a claim
An anticanonical projective hypersurface with trivial canonical bundle, lying at the critical degree d=n+2 rather than being used to refute a claim.
It is the adjunction-critical hypersurface degree where the canonical bundle becomes trivial, used illustratively rather than adversarially.

examples.name: Quartic surface in P^3
examples.note: The quartic in projective 3-space is mentioned as a contrasting case where the degree hypothesis for the non-extension statement is needed, since many objects do extend from the ambient infinitesimal neighborhood.
examples.engagement_reason: the quartic surface in P^3 is only invoked as a contrast where objects can extend to the bigger space, with no construction or computation carried out

passes.reason: A quartic surface in P^3 is a K3/Calabi–Yau boundary hypersurface and is invoked to show the non-extension statement fails without the degree assumption.
a quartic surface in P^3 is a K3/Calabi–Yau boundary hypersurface and is invoked to show the non-extension statement needs the degree hypothesis.
A quartic surface in P^3 is the Calabi–Yau/K3 boundary degree case and is cited to show the non-extension statement needs the degree hypothesis.
A quartic surface in P^3 is the K3/Calabi–Yau boundary case and is cited to show the non-extension statement needs the degree hypothesis.
A quartic surface in P^3 is the K3/Calabi–Yau boundary case and is cited to show the nonextension statement fails without the low-degree assumption.
A quartic surface in P^3 is the K3/Calabi–Yau boundary case, and it is used to show the nonextension statement fails without the degree hypothesis.
It is invoked to show the non-extension obstruction statement fails without the degree assumption, and quartic surfaces are the Calabi–Yau boundary in P^3.
The quartic surface in P^3 is invoked to show the non-extension statement needs the degree hypothesis, and it is the Calabi–Yau/K3 boundary hypersurface in P^3.
The quartic K3 surface is cited to show the nonextension statement fails without the degree hypothesis, and it is the Calabi–Yau boundary degree in P^3.
the quartic K3 surface is invoked to show the low-degree non-extension statement would fail without its hypothesis, and it is the critical K=0 degree among surfaces in P3
A quartic surface in P^3 is the Calabi–Yau/K3 boundary case and is invoked to show the small-degree nonextension statement would fail without its hypothesis.
A quartic surface in P^3 is a K3 hypersurface at the Calabi–Yau degree boundary, and it is cited to show the non-extension statement needs the degree assumption.

examples.name: Cubic sevenfold and its rigid Calabi–Yau mirror
examples.note: The cubic sevenfold is presented as the next tempting open target for the method, with its Kuznetsov component a Calabi–Yau 3 category and an old mirror-symmetry relation to a rigid Calabi–Yau threefold quotient of a product of elliptic curves.
examples.engagement_reason: the cubic sevenfold and its rigid Calabi–Yau mirror are cited as a motivating next case and known mirror-symmetry example, with only stated properties

passes.reason: The cited mirror is rigid, hence at the zero-deformation extreme among Calabi–Yau threefolds, while the example is posed as a prospective application rather than a disproof.
the mirror is rigid, so it has extremal zero complex deformations, and the pair is presented as an open Torelli target rather than as a refutation.
The cubic sevenfold is proposed as the next open Torelli case, while its Calabi–Yau mirror is rigid, i.e. at minimal deformation dimension, not a refuting example.
The mirror is a rigid Calabi–Yau threefold, extremal in having no complex deformations, and the example is presented as an open next case rather than a refutation.
The rigid Calabi–Yau mirror has minimal deformation dimension, while the example is presented as a next open application rather than as a refutation.

The cubic sevenfold is a standard hypersurface family member while its rigid Calabi–Yau mirror has minimal deformation dimension, and the pair is presented as an open next case rather than a refutation.
It is presented as the next open test case, with an extremal rigid Calabi–Yau mirror having no complex deformations.
The cubic sevenfold with its rigid Calabi–Yau mirror is presented as a special open/application case rather than a refutation, with rigidity giving an extremal Calabi–Yau mirror.
The cubic sevenfold/rigid Calabi–Yau mirror is presented as a prospective next application rather than a refutation, while the mirror is rigid with minimal deformation dimension.
it is presented as the next open Torelli/mirror-symmetry case, with an extremal rigid Calabi–Yau mirror having no complex deformations
The cubic sevenfold is presented as the next open systematic case, while its rigid Calabi–Yau mirror is extremal by having zero-dimensional deformation space.
